summary:
The Callanish Stones are a cruciform arrangement of standing stones on the Isle of Lewis in the Outer Hebrides, Scotland, erected around 2900–2600 BCE. The central circle of thirteen stones surrounds a monolith nearly five meters tall, from which four avenues of smaller stones radiate outward. The layout has astronomical alignments, most notably with the southernmost rising of the moon every 18.6 years, suggesting the site served calendrical and ritual functions for the island's Neolithic communities.

The monument required the quarrying and transport of Lewisian gneiss, one of the oldest rocks on Earth, over considerable distances. Excavations have revealed a chambered tomb predating the stone avenues at the center of the circle, indicating the site was a place of ancestor veneration before the main construction. The Callanish complex represents the northernmost expression of a megalithic tradition that extended across the British Isles and western Europe during the fourth and third millennia BCE.
